MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=1] A(1): The paper provides no comparisons against any baselines (summary: "Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so there is no empirical grounding to assess relative performance. Because no prior methods are evaluated, fairness of comparison, acknowledgment when prior work performs comparably, and analysis of differences cannot be established—despite adequate related-work citation, the empirical comparison component is effectively missing. | B(1): The paper does not compare against appropriate baselines — no prior multimodal or image-caption agreement models are evaluated alongside the proposed framework (only a single generic multimodal neural net is tested). Because key baseline comparisons are missing, fairness of comparisons and analysis of differences cannot be assessed, even though the paper provides reproducible code and hyperparameter details. While related work appears discussed, the experimental evaluation lacks meaningful prior-method comparisons, which is critical for situating the contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides no comparisons against any baselines (summary: "Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so there is no empirical grounding to assess relative performance. Because no prior methods are evaluated, fairness of comparison, acknowledgment when prior work performs comparably, and analysis of differences cannot be established—despite adequate related-work citation, the empirical comparison component is effectively missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper does not compare against appropriate baselines — no prior multimodal or image-caption agreement models are evaluated alongside the proposed framework (only a single generic multimodal neural net is tested). Because key baseline comparisons are missing, fairness of comparisons and analysis of differences cannot be assessed, even though the paper provides reproducible code and hyperparameter details. While related work appears discussed, the experimental evaluation lacks meaningful prior-method comparisons, which is critical for situating the contribution.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the introduction, method (microworld generation and DMRS-based captions), experiments on multiple controlled tasks, and conclusions follow a clear logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good; notation and definitions appear consistent and hyperparameters and code availability are documented. Figures, tables, and concrete task examples (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) are informative and help convey experimental design, though some sections could benefit from slightly more explicit signposting of how experimental choices link to claims about compositional generalization. From my perspective as a theory-focused reader, clarity is high but minor improvements in tying design decisions to evaluation goals would make the presentation exemplary. | B(4): The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation to method (SHAPEWORLD microworld generation with DMRS-based captions) to experiments on several controlled tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) and conclusion. Main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good; notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and reproducible (code and hyperparameters provided). Figures, tables and example captions are informative for diagnosing compositional generalization, and related work is adequately covered. Minor presentation niggles remain (the closed-world, small vocabulary limitation and artificiality are noted but could use a slightly clearer discussion of boundaries), so it stops short of being exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and readable: the introduction, method (microworld generation and DMRS-based captions), experiments on multiple controlled tasks, and conclusions follow a clear logical flow. The main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is reported as good; notation and definitions appear consistent and hyperparameters and code availability are documented. Figures, tables, and concrete task examples (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) are informative and help convey experimental design, though some sections could benefit from slightly more explicit signposting of how experimental choices link to claims about compositional generalization. From my perspective as a theory-focused reader, clarity is high but minor improvements in tying design decisions to evaluation goals would make the presentation exemplary.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized and follows a clear flow from motivation to method (SHAPEWORLD microworld generation with DMRS-based captions) to experiments on several controlled tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) and conclusion. Main contributions are stated early and the writing quality is good; notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and reproducible (code and hyperparameters provided). Figures, tables and example captions are informative for diagnosing compositional generalization, and related work is adequately covered. Minor presentation niggles remain (the closed-world, small vocabulary limitation and artificiality are noted but could use a slightly clearer discussion of boundaries), so it stops short of being exceptional.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important problem and contributes a practical, controllable synthetic framework (SHAPEWORLD) that exposes concrete failure modes in multimodal compositional generalization. Its value lies in being a reproducible diagnostic tool likely to spur follow-up work, but impact is constrained by the closed-world, small-vocabulary setup and lack of real-data transfer or theoretical breakthroughs. Given these strengths and limitations, a score of 4 reflects strong applied utility without claiming transformative real-world impact.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important problem—probing multimodal compositional generalization—by providing a synthetic microworld (SHAPEWORLD) that can generate controlled image+caption agreement data and hold out configuration constraints. This is a useful tooling contribution that can expose specific failure modes of generic multimodal DNNs and thus opens concrete experimental directions. However, its impact is limited by the closed-world, small-vocabulary synthetic domain, lack of baselines or real-data transfer validation, and the absence of theoretical or algorithmic advances that would change how researchers think about generalization in realistic settings.

reviewer_b.justification: This work tackles an important problem—diagnosing compositional generalization in multimodal models—by providing a controllable synthetic framework (SHAPEWORLD) with code and multiple targeted tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) that let researchers hold out specific configurations at train time. As an applied researcher I value its practical, reproducible setup and diagnostic image-caption agreement task: it reveals concrete failure modes where models achieve high training performance but fail to recombine held-out combinations. While synthetic and limited in linguistic scope, the framework is likely to be widely used for focused probing and will open useful research directions for improving compositional generalization, though it stops short of being fully transformative for real-world language–vision problems.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the contribution is a useful, practical framework for controlled, compositional evaluation rather than a new algorithm or deep theoretical advance. The work’s DMRS-based generation, targeted tasks, and held-out configuration protocol represent a meaningful and reusable novelty for multimodal diagnostics, but the idea is incremental relative to prior synthetic benchmarks and lacks real-data transfer or algorithmic innovation. For these reasons a strong-but-not-top originality score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper offers a moderately novel contribution in the form of a synthetic microworld framework (SHAPEWORLD) that generates DMRS-based captions and supports controlled hold-out configurations to probe compositional generalization. However, the core contribution is primarily a dataset/framework rather than a new algorithm or theoretical advance, and the evaluation is limited to synthetic image-caption agreement tasks without baselines or real-data transfer evidence. The generation/holdout idea and use of small closed vocabularies echo prior synthetic benchmarks, so the novelty is incremental rather than a surprising advance for experts seeking formal or algorithmic innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: As an applied multimodal researcher I find SHAPEWORLD a notable and useful novelty: it offers a controllable synthetic microworld and explicit held-out configuration protocol to probe compositional generalization in image-caption agreement tasks, which is a concrete perspective not common in standard captioning/visual QA datasets. The framework’s DMRS-based caption generation, multiple targeted tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER), and released code enable reproducible, fine-grained diagnostic evaluation beyond off-the-shelf benchmarks. This is more than a straightforward reuse of existing datasets but stops short of a radically new algorithmic technique, so it merits a strong but not top-tier originality score.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides a well-designed, reproducible synthetic framework with multiple controlled tasks and clear diagnostic value, and both note the explicit discussion of limitations. The primary shortcoming is the lack of baseline comparisons and ablation/sensitivity analyses to strengthen causal claims and real-data validation; these are important but do not outweigh the solid empirical protocol and useful negative results, so a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The experimental suite covers several controlled tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) and reports hyperparameters and code, so reproducibility is decent; limitations of the closed vocabulary/synthetic domain are explicitly discussed. However, no baseline comparisons or real-data transfer experiments are presented to support claims about generalization beyond the microworlds, and no ablation/sensitivity studies are reported tying design choices to observed failures. Related work coverage is reported as adequate. From a theory-oriented perspective the work is useful as a diagnostic framework but lacks the empirical and analytical depth (ablations, baselines, real-data validation) required to fully support its broader claims.

reviewer_b.justification: This work provides a well-designed, reproducible synthetic framework (code and hyperparameters available) and multiple controlled tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) that reveal clear failure modes and compositional generalization gaps, supporting the main claims. The experiments are thorough in diagnostic coverage and discuss limitations of the closed-world, small-vocabulary setup, but the evaluation lacks comparisons to alternative architectures or explicit ablation/sensitivity studies that would strengthen causal claims about model components. Overall the empirical protocol, held-out configuration splits, and reported negative results make this a solid, practically useful contribution with only minor gaps in comparative/sensitivity analysis.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experimental framework is clear and supports the main empirical claims but is limited by closed-world synthetic data, lack of comparative baselines, absence of statistical testing, and no theoretical guarantees. Given these consistent methodological gaps and missing controls/ablations that reduce confidence in generalization, a moderate technical-soundness score is warranted. I therefore select 3 to reflect that the empirical work is sensible and reproducible but not sufficiently rigorous to merit higher confidence.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a clear experimental framework and supports its empirical claims with controlled synthetic experiments across multiple tasks, but there is no theoretical analysis or formal guarantees and no comparison to baselines. The evaluation is limited to closed-world synthetic data and I see no statistical testing or ablation studies tying design choices to observed failures, which weakens claims about generalization. Overall the methodology is sensible for probing compositional behaviour, but important controls and formal/theoretical support are missing, so technical confidence is moderate rather than high.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by controlled experiments demonstrating that SHAPEWORLD can probe compositional generalization via held-out configuration splits and that models fail to recombine training primitives. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the implementation appears reproducible (code, hyperparameters provided). However, the experimental methodology is weakened by lack of comparative baselines and no mention of statistical tests, and the closed-world/synthetic assumptions limit broader claims about real-world generalization. Overall the technical contribution and empirical diagnosis are sound, with only minor methodological gaps that do not overturn the conclusions.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper presents a relevant, well-engineered synthetic benchmark with reusable code and controlled tasks that address compositional generalization in multimodal settings, making it appropriate for the target community. Reviewer A’s concerns about limited real-world validation, absence of algorithmic/theoretical advances, and lack of baselines warrant tempering enthusiasm, so a slightly conservative score reflects strong fit but not a fully comprehensive contribution expected at the highest bar.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a core multimodal/language-understanding topic that is relevant to top ML/NLP venues (synthetic microworlds to probe compositional generalization in image+caption tasks) and provides code and multiple controlled tasks. However, from my theoretical-perspective it is largely an empirical/dataset contribution with no baselines, no formal definitions or guarantees about the generalization claims, and evaluations are exclusively synthetic without transfer to real data—weakening its appeal for a top venue that increasingly expects either provable insights or real-world validation. Its positioning is reasonable for the community, but the lack of algorithmic/theoretical advances or external validation makes it borderline rather than a clear fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces SHAPEWORLD, a reproducible synthetic microworld for controlled multimodal evaluation that directly targets compositional generalization—an important open problem in vision+language research. The work provides concrete diagnostic tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER), code availability, and evaluation protocols that the community can reuse to probe model failure modes, matching the audience and methodological expectations of such conferences. While the domain is intentionally simplified and the vocabulary is small (acknowledged limitations), that focused design is precisely the strength for creating controlled, interpretable benchmarks for multimodal generalization.

main_claims: Introduce SHAPEWORLD, a synthetic microworld framework to automatically generate multimodal (image + caption) data for evaluating language understanding and generalization of models.

Use an image-caption agreement task on controlled, generated microworlds of colored shapes to probe specific linguistic capabilities and compositional generalization by holding out configuration constraints during training.

Demonstrate the framework by evaluating a generic multimodal deep neural network on multiple SHAPEWORLD tasks (ONESHAPE, MULTISHAPE, SPATIAL, QUANTIFIER) and show that the framework yields insights into model capabilities and failure modes.
_summary.methodology: Automatically generate synthetic microworld images of colored shapes paired with DMRS-based captions, then evaluate models on an image-caption agreement task where test constraints (e.g., shape-color combinations, counts, spatial relations, caption types) can be held out during training to force compositional generalization.
limitations: Closed-world domain with a small/closed vocabulary (stated as far less than 100 words) limiting linguistic variety.
Artificial/simulated data may not capture complexities of real-world language and vision; language generation difficulty increases with world complexity.
Models can achieve optimal training performance yet fail evaluation if held-out configurations are not generalized to (i.e., requires true recombination/generalization).

reviewer_a.name: Theory Purist
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning theory and formal semantics with a history of work on generalization bounds and compositionality proofs for language models.
traits: Penalises empirical-only papers that lack formal definitions or theoretical analysis (e.g., no generalization bounds, minimal formal semantics).
Demands real-world validation or transfer experiments for claims about 'generalization'; deducts points if evaluation is exclusively synthetic without demonstrating relevance to natural data.
Requires strong algorithmic or theoretical novelty beyond benchmark construction; deducts when contribution is mainly a dataset/framework unless accompanied by provable insights or algorithms.
Scores down papers that omit ablation studies tying design choices to observed behaviors (expects explicit causal/ablation evidence).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether a paper advances theoretical understanding or provides formal guarantees; is harsh on empirical benchmarks that lack proofs, real-data validation, or algorithmic advances.

reviewer_b.name: Benchmark Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Applied researcher in multimodal learning and evaluation, with experience building benchmarks and analyzing failure modes of vision-language systems.
traits: Rewards well-designed synthetic benchmarks that enable fine-grained, controllable probing of model capabilities and compositional generalization.
Gives credit for reproducibility: clear data-generation code, evaluation scripts, and multiple diagnostic tasks increase score significantly.
Values empirical diagnosis of model failures and insightful experimental protocols (e.g., held-out configurations, targeted splits); will upscore papers that reveal where current models fail.
Is forgiving of lack of formal theory provided the empirical setup yields actionable insights and a publicly usable framework for the community.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes practical contributions that enable better evaluation and diagnosis of models; favors clear, usable benchmarks and thorough empirical analyses even without formal proofs.
